text: Psalm 11 and a few weeks ago on Wednesday evenings, of course, this is the first Wednesday service that we've had in a couple of weeks because we had Christmas Eve service on a Tuesday night, New Year's Eve service on a Tuesday night. And prior to that, however, we'd started a little mini series here in Psalm 11 and we had finished up one series that we're dealing with, doing what we ought to do. And before we transition into our next series on the names of God, we're looking at Psalm 11 here and seeking to just be encouraged. And we got stuck in verse number one several weeks ago. And the reason why is because the Psalmist declares in the Lord, put I my trust. We're gonna answer the question, why is it that he would make the decision? Why is it that we should make the decision to put, to place our trust in the Lord? And what does it mean when we put our trust in the Lord? What are the blessings? What are the results of putting our trust in the Lord? And we dealt with that for a couple of different weeks. And then we focused in our last study at the end of verse number one, how that he says, how say ye to my soul, flee as a bird to your mountain. He's declared that he's placed, he's put his trust in the Lord, yet there were some that were saying flee. All of these circumstances that you're dealing with, you should flee. And he says, how say ye to my soul, flee as a bird to your mountain. And I mentioned as we studied that portion of scripture that we need to be careful who we listen to, because there are some that will discourage us from trusting the Lord. And they'll discourage us from putting our trust in the Lord. They'll tell us all of the reasons logically and going through the process of trying to analyze something and the analytical minded people and say, no, no, no, you have to follow this. You can't trust that. And the reality is that David says in the Lord, put I my trust. As we begin focusing on verse number two this evening, we find that David would continue to trust the Lord, which would result in rest. And what a blessing to know that we're in the hands of God, that I've put my trust in the Lord, Jehovah God, the self-existent one, the one who said, let there be light and there is light. But verse number two, as we begin to unfold this Psalm, enables us to understand with greater clarity, the circumstances that David was dealing with. Notice what the Bible says in verse number two, it'll be our focus of our study this evening. The Bible says for lo, the wicked bend their bow, they make ready their arrow upon the string that they may privilege shoot at the upright in heart. In verse number two, we find that there are two categories of people that are referenced to us. The first is the wicked. We could also refer to them as being the ungodly. In fact, throughout the Psalms, you'll find that those words sometimes, many times are used interchangeably. And we find that he speaks about the wicked in verse number two. And he then also speaks about the upright in heart. If we would say that the wicked are the ungodly, we would say that the upright in heart are the righteous. And so we find that there's these two categories of people that are introduced to us in verse number two. And as we study this verse of scripture this evening, I've simply titled the message, the thought this evening under attack. I wonder, do you ever feel like you're under attack? You say, man, every day, all the time I feel like I'm under attack. And this is right in my wheelhouse. You're dealing with what I'm facing today. And the reality is that there are times in which seasons in which it seems like we're constantly being bombarded, that we're under attack. We understand that we have an enemy, but as we look at this text of scripture, we're reminded that it's not simply the enemy, although he is actively seeking whom he may devour, but there is a great contrast. There's a great conflict in the world between the ungodly or the wicked and the upright in the heart or the righteous. And we'll look at some things that Jesus said in the gospel of John, as we make our way through the message this evening. But as we look at this text of scripture, verse two and consider under attack, I want you to see number one, if you're taking notes, a plan uncovered, a plan uncovered. Why is it that we deal with this? And why do I use the verbiage that I use as a plan being uncovered? Well, because the reality is that though our enemy is subtle and though he is clever, though the wicked are operating with subtlety here, it talks about being may privilege shoot at the upright in heart. We understand that God has exposed or uncovered the plan of the wicked. We find that God has told us, even as we mentioned before in Psalm two, where the Bible talks about those that have gathered themselves together, the heathen rage and the people imagine a vain thing and how they take counsel together against the Lord and all that takes place. And so God has uncovered the plan of the enemy. And we find that as we look throughout the scriptures, in fact, the part of the text that I'll be preaching on this upcoming Sunday morning in first Corinthians chapter two, Paul says this, that we're not ignorant of his devices, speaking about the methodologies, if you will, of the enemy. As you study through the scriptures, it's amazing to find the many ways in which God reveals or exposes to us, uncovers the plan of the enemy. Notice in the verse of scripture, it says this, when we talk about their plan, we see that they're prepared. The Bible says that the wicked bend their bow. Not only do they bend their bow, but notice it says they make ready their arrow upon the string and they are prepared. They have bent their bow. They've made ready their arrows. The Bible continues to say that they may privily shoot at the upright in heart. We find that there are times in which it seems like in certain arenas of our lives, in certain circumstances, and David, we're getting ready to look at some things that David was facing personally and why he shared with us this truth this evening, but we find that this plan is being uncovered. It's being exposed. And we find that there are times in which we live and it seems like the enemy is just actively watching and waiting for just one misstep so that they can pounce. We're under a microscope, it seems like at times in our lives. We find here that this plan is uncovered. David was aware. He had already declared that in the Lord put on my trust. And he says that the wicked, they bend their bow. They make ready their arrow upon the string. They may privily shoot at the upright in heart. As we'd say that there's a plan uncovered, I would say number two, and we'll spend the majority of our time here this evening, a principle understood, a principle understood. As we look at this verse of scripture, we find that God uncovers their plan, but then he explains to us and helps us to understand a biblical principle that is so vital for us to incorporate in our lives, that as we walk this Christian life, that God would encourage us and enable us to understand the spiritual warfare that we're truly engaged in. We find that this principle understood, I'll make two thoughts with regard to the principle, share some verses of scripture, and then show it, illustrate it in the life of David. But notice this principle understood, it has two aspects to it. Number one, the wicked attack, and number two, the Lord defends. The wicked attack, but the Lord defends. And I'm so thankful that God is my rock. He is my defense, he's my fortress. I'm thankful this evening, when we talk about the Lord, he's my shield, he's a strong tower, I'm thankful for the defense. The Bible speaks about safety is of the Lord. And what a blessing to understand that though David was in this situation, and though the wicked had bent their bow, they had made ready their arrow upon the string, they sought to privily shoot at the upright in heart, that the reality is that our God provides defense. Now, let me be careful to try to explain something this evening that it's imperative that we understand. The righteous, the upright in heart, they do not hate the world, the ungodly, the wicked. It's the world that hates the righteous. It's the ungodly or the wicked that hates the righteous. And it's because they don't love the Lord. It's because they don't belong to the Lord.
